mainEntity.name: Is street photography legal in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland — photographing people in public places is lawful, no consent or model release required for editorial, artistic, or personal use. UK is one of the more permissive jurisdictions globally. France, Germany, Hungary stricter.

mainEntity.name: Do I need permission to photograph strangers?

acceptedAnswer.text: Legally no in UK public spaces for editorial/artistic/personal use. Commercial use (advertising, brand) typically requires model releases. Ethically more nuanced. Many street photographers ask after the fact rather than before, especially for portrait-style frames.

mainEntity.name: What if someone tells me to delete the photo?

acceptedAnswer.text: Legally no-one can compel deletion without court order. Practically, deleting is usually the right answer — memory of confrontation lasts longer than absence of a frame. Stay calm, be honest, show the frame, delete if requested, move on.

mainEntity.name: Is it OK to photograph children in public?

acceptedAnswer.text: Legally yes; ethically charged. Position most thoughtful street photographers take: never as identifiable subjects, never alone in frame as focus. Children appear only as part of wider scenes — silhouettes, blurred motion, incidental. If parent objects, delete and explain.

mainEntity.name: What's the best lens for UK street photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: 28mm or 35mm prime. 35mm is canonical Cartier-Bresson focal length — wide enough for context, tight enough for intimacy. 28mm pushes closer for more contextual frames. Avoid telephoto for street — 70-200mm from across road is rarely strongest work and often less honest.

mainEntity.name: Can I publish street photos of identifiable people on Instagram?

acceptedAnswer.text: Legally yes for editorial/artistic/personal use. Commercial use greyer — may need model release. Modern social media has shifted ethics — frame subject didn't object to may come back to them years later via algorithm. Test: would I be comfortable if they found and contacted me?

mainEntity.name: Where can I photograph in central London?

acceptedAnswer.text: Public streets, squares, parks, Thames riverside. Private-but-publicly-accessible spaces (Canary Wharf, Covent Garden, More London, South Bank near RFH, shopping centres) allow photography until asked to stop. Trafalgar tripod work needs permit. Calm explanation usually resolves challenges.

mainEntity.name: How do I overcome the fear of photographing strangers?

acceptedAnswer.text: Three approaches: start in busy tourist areas (Covent Garden, Borough Market, South Bank); work urban-geometry first to get camera out without people as central subject; settle ethical framework before going out. Fear usually evaporates within 3-4 sessions once code is worked out.
